claims.claimText: Carpal tunnel syndrome evaluation combines the symptom pattern, focused median nerve examination, and selective diagnostic testing rather than relying on routine imaging.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; source before manufacture

claims.claimText: Carpal tunnel syndrome localizes to the median nerve within the carpal tunnel; release planning also requires review of median nerve branch anatomy.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; attending anatomy review required

claims.claimText: Document sensory distribution, thenar motor findings, and provocative maneuvers while considering proximal median neuropathy, cervical radiculopathy, and other mimics.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; source before manufacture

claims.claimText: Electrodiagnostic testing can help confirm CTS, localize competing diagnoses, and characterize severity when results will affect management; routine MRI or radiographs should not be presented as the standard CTS diagnostic or follow-up pathway.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; EDX specialist review required

claims.claimText: CTS treatment planning should distinguish neutral-position night orthosis, corticosteroid injection with short-term rather than durable benefit framing, and named open or endoscopic carpal tunnel release procedures without implying universal superiority of either release technique.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; attending review required

claims.claimText: Persistent symptoms after release, recurrent symptoms after a period of improvement, incomplete transverse carpal ligament release, pillar pain, and procedure-related median or branch nerve injury should be modeled as distinct post-release concepts unless curator review intentionally links or merges them.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; attending review required

claims.claimText: After carpal tunnel release, activity, therapy, and return-to-work guidance should be individualized; routine postoperative wrist immobilization, routine supervised therapy, and repeat radiographs are not default CTS follow-up requirements.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; attending and rehabilitation review required

claims.claimText: OITE content should test median nerve branch anatomy, the limits of individual provocative maneuvers, NCS versus needle EMG roles, and the curriculum distinction between persistent symptoms, recurrent symptoms, and incomplete release without encoding an unsupported fixed recurrence interval.

metadata.sourceNote: CTS vertical preparation; educator and domain review required

decisionPoints.trigger: Atypical presentation, uncertain localization or severity, competing diagnosis, preoperative severity question, or a result expected to change management.
decisionPoints.action: Consider targeted electrodiagnostic testing and avoid defaulting to routine radiographs or MRI for typical CTS evaluation.

decisionPoints.trigger: CTS presentation without progressive thenar weakness, denervation concern, acute traumatic compression, or another reviewed surgical escalation indication.
decisionPoints.action: Discuss education, neutral-position night orthosis, and/or corticosteroid injection using source-specific expectations and follow-up; frame injection as short-term symptom relief rather than durable disease modification.

decisionPoints.trigger: Persistent symptoms after failed nonoperative treatment, severe disease, or objective thenar weakness, thenar atrophy, or denervation findings.
decisionPoints.action: Discuss a named carpal tunnel release option after confirming diagnosis, severity, patient factors, and goals; use expedited non-emergent evaluation for thenar weakness, atrophy, or denervation findings, while persistent symptoms after failed nonoperative treatment remain a routine surgical discussion.

decisionPoints.trigger: A reviewed surgical indication is present and open versus endoscopic technique is being selected.
decisionPoints.action: Teach open and endoscopic release as equivalent named treatment options; select through shared decision-making informed by anatomy, prior surgery, risks, resources, patient preference, and surgeon expertise. Do not encode a default operative pathway.

decisionPoints.trigger: Visualization is inadequate or anatomy is unsafe or uncertain during endoscopic release.
decisionPoints.action: Stop further endoscopic release and convert to an open approach when appropriate according to local protocol and supervising surgeon judgment. Do not encode mandatory conversion.

decisionPoints.trigger: Failure of CTS symptoms to adequately improve following carpal tunnel release.
decisionPoints.action: Reassess diagnosis, localization, severity, and release completeness before considering revision. Do not require a minimum postoperative interval or proof of incomplete release.

decisionPoints.trigger: CTS symptoms return after a documented period of postoperative improvement following release.
decisionPoints.action: Evaluate recurrent compression and alternative causes before considering revision. Do not require a fixed symptom-free interval.

decisionPoints.trigger: Progressive neurologic deficit, vascular concern, wound concern, or severe unexpected postoperative pain after carpal tunnel release.
decisionPoints.action: Escalate promptly to the treating surgical team for assessment under the local postoperative protocol.
